title: Lesson 1: Loving God
description: The Father runs toward you first. This lesson introduces the Greatest Commandment as a response to God's initiating love: receive His love, then respond with your whole self.

blocks.text: Welcome to the Growing Courses

The 200-level path has three connected courses: Growing Deep (201), Growing Up (202), and Growing Out (203). Together they show the shape of a growing life: deep roots in the gospel, strong character in daily life, and visible fruit in love for others.

Growing Deep begins with your most important relationship: the vertical one between you and God. His love comes down, and your love for Him and others is a response. The title of this lesson carries a double meaning, and both sides matter. God is a loving God who pursues you, and loving God back is what you were made to do.

blocks.alt: A father lifting his robe and running down the road to embrace his returning son

blocks.title: The Running Father
blocks.text:
Jesus once told a story about a father and his two sons. Most people know it as the Parable of the Prodigal Son, but the father is the picture we need to see first.

The younger son asked for his inheritance early, which was like saying, "I wish you were dead." Then he left home and wasted everything. When he finally came to himself, he started back with a speech ready, hoping to be treated like a servant.

"But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him." (Luke 15:20)

The father did not wait for the speech. He ran. He embraced him. He kissed him. Then came the robe, the ring, and the feast. Before the son could earn anything back, the father welcomed him home.

This is the heart of God toward you. Not patient tolerance. Not cautious acceptance. A Father running to bring His child home.

Hold on to the image of that robe the father wrapped around his son. It is more than welcome-home clothing. It is a picture of something Jesus was about to do, and we will come back to it in the next lesson.
1.1 - When you picture the Father running toward you, not waiting for a speech, not waiting for you to clean up, what stirs inside you?
Because He Loved First

This is why, when Jesus was asked what matters most in all of life, He did not answer with a philosophy or a program. He went back to the oldest prayer in Israel, the words faithful families had prayed morning and evening for a thousand years. It is called the Shema (pronounced "sheh-MAH"), and it means "hear."

Jesus answered, "The most important is, 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one. And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength.'"

Heart, soul, mind, and strength are not four separate compartments. They are four ways of saying your whole self. Your deepest desires, the core of who you are, your thoughts, your energy, all of it. Nothing held back. It is the highest command Jesus ever gave, and if we are honest it sounds impossible. How do you love anyone that completely, let alone an invisible God?
Here is the part that changes everything. You do not have to generate this kind of love from nothing.
We love because he first loved us.

Read that short verse again. We love because He first loved us. The command to love God is not asking you to produce something you do not have. It is inviting you to respond to something already given. Say this line until it sinks all the way in: I do not love God to make Him love me. I love God because He loved me first. That single confession is the hinge the whole Christian life swings on. Every morning, every act of obedience, every return from drifting begins there.

This is the simple rhythm of the Christian life: receive and respond. First, you receive the Father's love. Then you respond with love for Him and for others. Love does not begin with you trying harder. It begins with letting God's love come to you before you try to give love back.

When that love comes down and lands in your heart, it does not stay there. It moves. The diagram below walks you through the shape of that love, how it comes from God to you, how it returns from you to Him, and how it finally overflows from you to the people around you. Tap "Begin" and walk through each stage slowly.

This is the Greatest Commandment, and it is shaped like a cross for a reason. The vertical beam is the love between God and you, His coming down and your responding up. The horizontal beam is where that love overflows into the people God has placed near you. You cannot get to the horizontal without first receiving the vertical. Which means the whole Christian life begins not with what you do for God, but with what He has already done for you.

That is very different from the way many people picture God. A boss watches your work and measures your performance. A father watches the road and longs for you to come home. If you see God mainly as a boss, obedience will feel like proving yourself. If you receive Him as Father, obedience becomes a grateful response to the One who already called you His child.

One last word before we close this first lesson. The Father who runs is also the Father who gives. He did not only lift His robe and cross the distance of a dusty road. He gave the most precious thing He had so the road home would exist at all.
For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.

The Father loves you. The Son saves you. The Spirit fills you. In the next lesson you will meet the Son who was given for you, the One who made the road home possible. In the lesson after that you will meet the Spirit who lives inside you and guides your daily walk. These three lessons belong together, and this week is where you plant the root.
1.2 - Where do you tend to picture God more like a boss watching your performance than a Father watching the road? What would change if you received Him as Father first?

1.3 - Say this line out loud and sit with it for a moment: I do not love God to make Him love me. I love God because He loved me first. What would change in your life if this became the first sentence you believed every morning?
This Week's Practice

Each morning this week, before you check your phone, say this line out loud and sit with it for one minute: I do not love God to make Him love me. I love God because He loved me first. You do not need to write a paragraph or feel something profound. Just let the Father's love be the first thing that shapes your morning, before the noise rushes in. And somewhere this week, read the full story in Luke 15:11-32, this time with your eyes on the Father.
Lesson 1 Review

God loved you before you ever loved Him back. The whole Christian life begins there: receive His love, then respond with your whole self. You do not love God to make Him love you. You love God because He loved you first.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
